title: Way-too-early 2027 NFL Draft predictions for Penn State players
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Following a 2026 NFL Draft where eight former Penn State players were drafted, projections and predictions are already beginning for the highly anticipated 2027 draft class.

While many Nittany Lions will be playing in the blue and white for the first time, there’s still enough talent on the team to begin projecting draft stock — with plenty of room for both growth and decline.

Here’s a look at where some Penn Staters could get selected during next year’s draft in Washington D.C.

Marcus Neal Jr., safety, Round 2

As of right now, the first player likely to hear his name called is safety Marcus Neal Jr., as the junior was one of the leading tacklers for Iowa State last season with 77. He also had a sack and two interceptions.

Neal is a player who’ll be all over the field in 2026 for the Nittany Lions and can make plays at any level. With safeties becoming such versatile assets, there has been a newfound importance of drafting safeties in early rounds, with multiple safeties going in the first round the past couple of years.

Penn State has had a lot of recent success with safeties getting drafted into the pros, including Zakee Wheatley, Jaylen Reed, Kevin Winston Jr. and Ji’Ayir Brown. Neal could join those names next year as an early selection, and barring a breakout from one of his teammates, he very well could be the first Nittany Lion off the board.

Tony Rojas, linebacker, Round 3

Tony Rojas’ first few games back will give more indication as to where he could be drafted, considering he’s coming off a torn ACL. But the intangibles Rojas brings will surely allow him to rise up the board.

Rojas’ on-field talent and athleticism allows him to fully play the linebacker position both as a run stopper and in pass coverage. No matter where Rojas’ draft stock is in January, it’s likely to continue rising during the pre-draft process.

The redshirt junior can absolutely fly around the field and could be in major contention to set records for a 40-yard dash time as a linebacker. It wouldn’t be surprising if Rojas hits low 4.40s or even into the 4.30s at the NFL Scouting Combine or Penn State’s Pro Day.

Audavion Collins, cornerback, Day 2-3

Another player whose stock will naturally rise because of his speed is Audavion Collins. But it’s not just his speed that’ll push him up draft boards.

Collins is the likely top cornerback this upcoming season despite plenty of other talent at the position. The 2026 defensive back room could very well be one of the most talented units as a whole and as the leader Collins, could be in for a big year and a rise is in his draft stock.

While Collins’ coverage skills are strong and he’s incredibly fast, his size could be why he falls down the board. Collins is listed at 5-foot-11, 180 pounds, which isn’t a typical NFL body. However, multiple cornerbacks in the 2026 NFL Draft had similar builds and went early in the draft.

Rocco Becht, quarterback, Day 3

Rocco Becht’s stock is very uncertain, given his injuries from last year and a fresh start at Penn State.

Becht will enter his final year with some of the highest quarterback experience in the country. His on-field presence and leadership will certainly help his stock, and his strong accuracy could increase his chances of hearing his name called.

The comparisons to Sean Clifford, who drafted in the fifth round in 2023, have already begun for Becht. His numbers are eerily similar to the former Penn State quarterback, which puts his Day 3 projection in a good spot if those similarities remain.

Chase Sowell, wide receiver, Day 3

Likely the top receiver this upcoming season, Chase Sowell could be in for a big year under the same system he had last year.

Sowell was the No. 1 receiver at Iowa State last year, even with fellow transfer Brett Eskildsen outgaining him by 26 yards. With the talent that’s alongside Sowell, it’ll allow him to get one-on-one opportunities where he can showcase his own talents.

The redshirt senior received comparisons from his wide receiver coach Kashif Moore to that of 2026 NFL draftee Skyler Bell, who Moore coached and developed at UConn. If Moore can unleash that type of development in Sowell, the Nittany Lion could hear his name called even earlier.

Other names to watch

Penn State could have around eight players drafted again next year. Other names to watch include running back Carson Hansen, tight end Benjamin Brahmer, offensive lineman Anthony Donkoh and cornerback Zion Tracy.

Hansen will likely be in at least a duo, if not a trio, that’ll rotate playing time which could limit his production and lower his stock. Brahmer’s big frame and overall ability will likely get him drafted, while Donkoh’s consistency and versatility could boost his draft position.

Despite getting a lot of hype from Matt Campbell, Tracy’s stock will be hard to rise compared to other cornerbacks because of his nickel position. The nickel corner spot, albeit very impactful and important, isn’t a spot that teams often use higher draft picks on.

title: Column | Advice from a recent graduate
author: Emma Lessick | The Daily Collegian
description:
There’s something about graduating that suddenly makes me feel qualified to give life advice — despite the fact that only a few years ago, I was walking around campus in your shoes, an underclassman at the bottom of the food chain.

Regardless, in the spirit of growth (and mild delusion), here’s my completely unsolicited advice, most of which I had to learn the hard way. So, maybe just take a moment to hear me out.

First, romanticize your life. I'm serious. Put your headphones on, pretend you’re the main character and walk to class like you have somewhere important to be (even if it’s just a lecture you will half listen to). It doesn’t fix everything, but trying to enjoy the small things can make the day exponentially better. Also, it encourages you to enjoy the mundane, everyday things.

Go to office hours. Not because you’re an overachiever or because you have deeply intellectual questions, but because most professors are significantly more generous to students whose names they recognize. I don’t make the rules.

Also, stop taking 8 a.m. classes with the mindset of “I’ll just get over it.” You won’t. It will get you. There’s no version of you, no matter how motivated, rested or optimistic, that enjoys walking to class while the sun is still figuring out what it wants to do for the rest of the day.

Here’s another one. Canvas will humble you. Every time. You can have your entire week planned out, your life together, your notes color-coded and then suddenly it's 11:58 p.m. and you're submitting something you started only a little bit ago.

Those pesky notifications at whatever time of day or night always gave me cardiac arrhythmia in anticipation of whether it was a grade, message from my professor or a reminder for an assignment I’ve yet to turn in.

Now onto less academic stuff:

Text your friends back. Not eventually. Not “I’ll get to it later.” Later turns into never and then suddenly you're “so busy” and find your inner circle of friends shrinking by the day.

Take pictures of everything, even the random, boring moments. You think you’ll remember them. You won’t. At the end of your college years, your camera roll will end up meaning more to you than your GPA.

Learn how to be alone without feeling lonely. There will be nights when no one’s around, plans fall through or everyone else seems busy. Take yourself out. Go on a walk. Go get food. It’s not sad, it’s independence, self-care and a skill to be able to enjoy your own company.

Stop waiting to feel “put together.” No one wakes up one day and suddenly has their life perfectly aligned. Everyone is improvising. Some people just have better outfits while doing it (couldn’t be me).

Don’t stay in situations (or with people) that make you feel small. Whether it’s friendships, relationships or just the environment in general, if you constantly feel, or are being made to feel like you’re not enough, that’s your sign to leave. Protect your peace.

Likewise, let yourself outgrow people and places. It doesn’t have to mean anything dramatic happened. Sometimes you change, sometimes other people change, that’s allowed.

Say yes more than you say no, but know when to say no. Go out, try things, be spontaneous. But also, if you need a night in, take it. Missing one night out with your friends will not ruin your social life.

Drink water. Seriously. Not everything can be solved with iced coffee, vibes or alcohol. Stay hydrated with H2O.

Call your parents (or whoever feels like home). Even if it’s quick. Even if you think you don’t have much to say. One day, you’ll wish you did it more.

You will be OK. Even when things don’t go how you planned, even when you feel behind or have no idea what you’re doing next, you’ll figure it out. You always do.

If you take something from this, let it be this. College doesn’t end with some big moment where everything suddenly makes sense. It kind of just ends, and you're left with a camera roll full of blurry pictures and the absolute best memories.

And if all else fails, at least check Canvas before 11:59 p.m. Some habits are just worth keeping for a while.

title: How to celebrate Pride Month in State College
author: Saxtyn Lutz | The Daily Collegian
description:
During the month of June, communities across the world will be celebrating Pride Month, an observance and commemoration of LGBTQ+ culture.

State College hosts a variety of Pride events throughout June. Here are a few ways you can partake in the celebrations.

State College Pride Parade

Beginning at 10 a.m. on June 13, vehicles and community members will parade through the streets of downtown State College.

Vehicles begin at State College Area High School, will drive down Westerly Pikeway and then turn onto South Allen Street where marchers will follow them outside of the State College Municipal Building. Both vehicles and marchers will continue down South Allen Street, turn onto West College Avenue, then onto South Burrows Street and finally onto West Nittany Avenue.

From there, everyone will convene at the Sidney Friedman Park for the Pride Festival.

Those intending to participate in the parade can register here. Community members are also welcome to gather around the streets listed above to enjoy the parade.

State College Pride 2026 Festival

From noon to 4 p.m., a festival with live music, drag, community and vendors will be hosted by CentreLGBT+ Grand Marshal Mayor Erica Deuso of Downingtown, Pennsylvania.

Over the course of the festival, multiple performers will take to the stage, including Margot Bevington, Diego Man-Dana, Thomasin Grace, Porch Party, Eric Ian Farmer and Friends and Tempest Productions.

The festival will also include a variety of food vendors to satisfy summer-day cravings, including ice cream, lemonade and barbecue.

State College Pride After Party

The celebration doesn’t end once the festival is over. At 7 p.m., those who are 21+ can head to The Basement for an after party hosted by CentreLGBT+. This event will have specialty Pride drinks, photo-ops, music from DJ Timeless, dancing, community and celebration.

There is a $10 cover fee.

Youth Gayla

The Youth Gayla, hosted by CentreLGBT+, will be held from 7 p.m. to 10 p.m. on June 20 at the Millbrook Marsh Nature Center. It is a prom-style event that aims to provide a safe space for LGBTQ+ youth in grades 9 through 12.

Attendees can enjoy music, snacks and photo-ops for this masquerade-themed event. There is a $20 admission fee.

Pride Storefront Competition

Between June 1 and June 31, CentreLGBT+ is hosting a State College Pride Window Display Contest.

Select stores downtown will decorate their storefront windows with Pride-themed decorations, and people can vote for their favorite display by scanning the QR code on each respective window throughout the month.

Participating stores include Lions Pride, Chumley’s, Downtown State College Improvement District, the State Theatre, Connections, 3 Dots Downtown and Yoga Lab.

Pets & Pride Photo Contest

CentreLGBT+ is hosting a Pets & Pride virtual photo contest where participants can submit a photo of their pet.

The 2025 winner was a smiling Dalmatian named Paris who had rainbow spots painted on her back. The deadline to submit a photo is June 16 by 5 p.m. Only one entry is allowed per household.

More information on submissions can be found here.

Queer Meet-up

Every Saturday between 3 p.m. and 5 p.m., the Print Factory in Bellefonte hosts an event catered toward teens and young adults who are queer, questioning or allies.

title: Attending NSO, essential purchases | Items for first-years to check off their summer to-do lists
author: Neel Ved | The Daily Collegian
description:
The 2026-27 academic year is right around the corner, and with it comes a new class of freshmen about to step foot on campus. College is exciting, but it can also feel overwhelming when you start tallying everything that needs to happen before move-in day.

Here are some important things to add to your summer to-do list before arriving at Penn State.

Attend NSO (New Student Orientation)

All first-year students are required to attend New Student Orientation (NSO). Here, students learn about what University Park offers students — and it’s also when you register for fall classes.

NSO has multiple days available throughout the summer. Students must complete the NSO tasklist, online academic consultation and course scheduling window, and attend an in-person NSO program.

File FAFSA

The Free Application for Federal Student Aid (FAFSA) is a form from the U.S. Department of Education used by colleges to determine eligibility for federal, state and institutional financial aid. FAFSA is also used to determine grants and work-study funds.

The form must be filled out every year.

Submit Immunization Records

All incoming first-years are required to submit immunization records. There may also be additional vaccination requirements for those living on campus.

To submit immunization records, head to the myUHS portal.

Select housing

First-year students at University Park are required to live on campus.

To choose housing, sign in to the eLiving system with your Penn State Access ID and fill out the HFS Contract.

Students are allowed to select a preference — East, Pollock, South, West, or North Halls — for their dorm, but this preference is not guaranteed. Submitting the housing contract early does not increase the chance of getting preferred housing.

Penn State does not use a survey to match roommates, students are allowed to choose if they would like to room with someone they know. The roommate request must be mutual.

Set Up Mobile id+ Card

Penn State now requires students to use a Mobile id+ card instead of a physical one.

To set it up, students are required to submit a photo of themselves or a valid government-issued photo ID or valid passport. After the picture loads, download the Transact eAccounts app and log in through your Penn State account. Then, add the ID to your phone’s wallet app.

Select Meal Plan

Students must log in to the eLiving website and choose one of the three Campus Meal Plan options:

Level 1 - $987, total charge of $2,737

Level 2 - $1,238, total charge of $2,988

Level 3 - $1,468, total charge of $3,218

Football Lottery

Students interested in attending football games should submit a request for student tickets through the Penn State Student Account Manager during their assigned class-specific window. The window typically opens in late July and closes early August.

Everyone will have the same chance of getting tickets no matter when the request is submitted.

Essential dorm items

Comfort and decor

Mattress toppers are a great way to make those way-too-firm dorm beds more comfortable. Make sure it’ll fit a twin or twin XL, the size of campus dorm mattresses.

Students should also bring sheets, a comforter, pillows and blankets. Try picking out your favorite colors or designs to make your dorm feel a little more at-home.

Many dorms on campus — like West, Pollock and South — are unrenovated, so many rooms do not have proper air conditioning. Having a fan on standby helps when the summer heat is sweltering those first few months in the fall, and later on in the spring semester.

To feel more at home, having a small rug is a great addition to a dorm room. Not only does it make those tiled floors a bit more comfortable, but it’s a great way to add some extra decoration to your room.

Overhead lights can be quite annoying during those late-night study sessions when you’re trying not to wake your roommate. Having a desk lamp is helpful to contain that light to your side of the room. And some warmer lighting is always good for extra coziness.

Extra things to have on hand

Of course, it’s necessary to have things to clean your room. A small, hand-held vacuum and swiffer jet is easily stored, and they come in super handy during Sunday cleaning sessions. You can also bring wipes to clean door handles, desks and other areas of the room that collect dust.

As many of the bathrooms in East, Pollock, South, West or North Halls have communal bathrooms, having shower slippers would be beneficial.

Umbrellas are also helpful to shield you from rain on your way to class, or as you make your way downtown with your friends.

Having cold medicine is important as students are living close to one another. And there’s always the dreaded frat flu.

Reusable water bottles are also nice to bring with you, as you can keep it on your nightstand for those 3 a.m. chugs or with you as you attend class. Most dorm buildings have water fountains throughout.

title: Gavin McKenna, Jaxon Cover and Pierce Mbuyi set to perform at 2026 NHL Combine
author: Kippi Hall | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions are making one last roar before this year's NHL Draft.

Forward Gavin McKenna, along with recruits Jaxon Cover and Pierce Mbuyi, will be attending the 2026 NHL Combine, which will run from Monday, June 1 to Friday, June 6 in Buffalo, New York.

McKenna, a Whitehorse, Yukon, native, is Penn State's first Big Ten Scoring Champion with 38 points consisting of 11 goals and 27 assists. His 2025-26 season set the single-season program record for assists with 36.

The 2026 Hobey Baker Award Finalist entered his freshman season at Penn State rated as the the No. 1 prospect for the 2026 NHL Draft.

Cover, a left wing for the London Knights in the Ontario Hockey League (OHL), committed to Penn State in April toting 52 points, 20 goals and 32 assists for the 2025-26 season. The 4-star recruit played in 67 games for the Knights.

Mbuyi, another left wing from Mississauga, Ontario, was named OHL Rookie of the Year last season after achieving 52 points, 29 goals and 23 assists in 63 games for the Attack. The 5-star recruit committed to Penn State in June of last year.

title: Penn State track and field's Hayley Kitching breaks 2-day old school record
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After setting the 800-meter record during the NCAA East First Round prelims on Thursday, Hayley Kitching has done it again.

Kitching broke her record for the second time this week during the 800m finals, running 1:59.15 to finish third overall and book her ticket to nationals.

The Coffs Harbour, Australia, native ran 1:59.60 during the prelims on Thursday to win the race, which was a school record then.

Kitching marks the fourth Nittany Lion to qualify in the 800m for the NCAA Championships as she'll compete in her third outdoor championships during her career.

title: Penn State baseball adds Fairfield outfielder transfer TJ Schmalzle
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a disappointing year, the Nittany Lions entered the transfer portal to retool their lineup.

With key holes already opened up via graduation and portal departures, such as Jack Porter and Nate Voss, coach Mike Gambino added his second incoming bat.

After earning a commitment from East Stroudsburg University's Shanley Walls, Gambino took another swing on a lower-prestige conference star in the form of Fairfield's TJ Schmalzle. The Lake Wallenpaupack. Pennsylvania native posted over a .280 batting average in his last two starting seasons with the Stags and became a real ironman, appearing in over 50 games per year.

However, last season's senior year campaign was a breakout year for Schmalzle, with a career high 11 home runs and 42 RBIs. He also improved all aspects of his slash line except his on-base percentage, which took a slight hit with lessened walk and heightened strikeout rates.

Nevertheless, he adds needed stability to centerfield, with a perfect fielding percentage last year, he'll slot in to captain the outfield in an attempt to help the Nittany Lions have a bounce-back year.

title: Penn State women's lacrosse midfielder Anna Salerno earns IWLCA Senior All-Star nod
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
A strong final collegiate season just got even better for midfielder Anna Salerno.

After four seasons at St. Mary's, the Bel Air, Maryland, native posted 29 points in 19 games in her graduate student year with the Nittany Lions, along with a team high three game winning goals.

After such a strong performance, Salerno earned second-team All-Big Ten honors. Still, the accolades continued as she is now set to participate in the IWLCA Senior All-Star Game for the Blue Team, led by John Hopkins coach Tim McCormack.

Salerno's participation in the 2026 All-Star Game marks another consecutive year that a blue and white player has been invited to the competition, after Ellie Hollin and Lauren Saltz earned the honor in 2025.

After being unselected in the latest Women's Lacrosse League draft, this game gives Salerno one last chance to compete against the best of the best and showcase her talents for the next level.

title: Penn State women's hockey adds DIII goalie transfer Savanna Muir
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Nittany Lions have made an addition between the pipes.

After program icon Katie DeSa finished her senior season, and Lauren Hernick entered the transfer portal, the blue and white have made an addition to the position group after some big losses.

Savanna Muir arrives from SUNY Cortland, where she saw no action over two years on the roster, yet the Elizabeth, Pennsylvania, native has a chance to return to form in her home state after a stellar career with the Pittsburgh Penguins Elite Youth teams.

Muir also saw appearances this year with the Bishop Kearney Selects in Rochester, New York, as a part of the 19U AAA U.S. Nationals. This season with the BK Selects, she compiled a 9-1 record over 11 starts, with 6 shutouts.

title: Penn State track and field's Jake Palermo sets 400m school record
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
For the first time in over 50 years, Penn State has a new 400-meter school record holder.

Sophomore Jake Palermo set the record in the 400m finals at the NCAA East First Round, running 45.19 seconds to finish 14th in the race.

Palermo previously tied for the No. 2 mark in school history with a time of 45.22 seconds, but took it a step further on Friday, marking the third school record at the qualifier meet.

While the Rochester, New York, native missed out on an NCAA Championship qualification by two places, he still broke a 51-year-old record at Penn State, last set by Mike Sands in 1975.

title: Penn State women's volleyball announces full 2026 schedule
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State is taking its talents primetime.

The Nittany Lions’ full nonconference and Big Ten schedule was released on Thursday. The slate features three neutral-site games and the inaugural conference tournament at the end of the year.

The year starts with the previously announced Spikes Under The Lights exhibition tournament at AT&T Stadium, before a three-match homestand at the Penn State Invitational to begin the year. Making the trip to Happy Valley will be UConn, High Point and Hofstra.

The first match away from Happy Valley for the blue and white will be in the historic Wrigley Field against Kentucky, which defeated Penn State last year. The Nittany Lions will stay in Chicago to face DePaul before heading back to the state to face Stanford at The Palestra, as the venue celebrates its centennial anniversary in 2026.

The Nittany Lions' four remaining out-of-conference games are highlighted by the Penn State Classic, where Tennessee and USF visit. The weekend classic was originally first confirmed as a part of the Volunteers’ schedule release.

Big Ten play begins with an away clash against Michigan on Sept. 25, before also taking on the Spartans two days later.

Nebraska marks the Nittany Lions’ first home conference opponent on Oct. 1, to kickoff a four-match home series.

Finishing out the highlights for the schedule will be the Big Ten Tournament from Nov. 20 to 26 at Fishers Event Center in Fishers, Indiana.

title: ‘Pay it forward’ | Penn State men’s basketball hosts annual Coaches vs. Cancer Golf Tournament
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
New and old faces of the Penn State men’s basketball community gathered in Walker Clubhouse at Penn State’s golf courses on Friday. Two rounds of golf were played with many members catching up with each other, all in support of a cause that dates back three decades for the team.

It was Penn State’s 29th-annual golf tournament for its chapter of Coaches vs. Cancer, a program that has the National Association of Basketball Coaches help the American Cancer Society defeat cancer. The Nittany Lions’ chapter recently passed $5 million total raised for the cause all time, highlighting the work Penn State has done.

“The way I was raised, we talk about all the time, is to pay it forward,” Mike Rhoades said. “You have these great opportunities in your life, for us sometimes around basketball, and you get these great opportunities and moments of your lives that come your way. You pay that back by helping others and serving others.”

The Penn State chapter of Coaches vs. Cancer was started in 1995 by former head coach Bruce Parkhill. The chapter’s annual golf tournament began two years later in 1997 by then-head coach Jerry Dunn, and the tradition has carried on to today.

Within Penn State’s chapter fundraising, the golf tournament has raised over $3.2 million to date, according to a recent news release. Over 425 golfers participated, and Rhoades said it’s been a lot of fun to meet everyone who shows up.

“Coach Parkhill and coach Dunn got this thing rolling, and usually great things in life start with people coming together with the ideas, and then following through on those ideas and getting people on board to make that impact,” Rhoades said. “Coach Parkhill started that, and the neat thing about all this is, they still to this day feel such a good part of it, and that's why they come back. Their families come back, former players come back, and that tells you a lot about those men.”

Program alum and former assistant coach Joe Crispin was back at Penn State for the golf tournament after becoming the head coach at Air Force earlier this offseason. He said the event has “always been an inspirational thing and a unification thing for the community” and he wants to come back for it every time he can.

The field of players also included brothers Dwayne Stephens and Jarrett Stephens. Dwayne was recently hired as a new assistant coach for the staff while Jarrett is an alum who played from 1995-2000.

“It's an event that's even more special to me. Jarrett and myself, our mom is a cancer survivor, so I know firsthand how it impacts a lot of people's lives and families,” Dwayne said. “Anytime I can do something or be a part of something that's going to benefit and help cancer, I'm all in.”

In addition to the golf tournament, festivities for Penn State’s Coaches vs. Cancer chapter this week included a reception Thursday night. There’s also an online auction happening that gives all proceeds to the chapter, including bidding for a dinner with Rhoades.

Through all the donations and events, Penn State’s chapter recently became the fourth Coaches vs. Cancer chapter to raise over $5 million.

“(It) says a lot about Penn State, our community here, and State College, and so many people being a part of it and impacted in a special way,” Rhoades said.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: 40 year anniversary special | Top plays, moments from 1986 national championship season
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State was dominant throughout the 1986 season, allowing it to play for the national title against Miami, in which it’d come out on top.

However, the Nittany Lions wouldn’t have been in the title game without specific moments throughout the season which showcased a championship-minded team that wound up standing atop the college football pedestal.

Here are some of the top plays and moments from the 1986 national championship season.

Early season test, victory vs. Cincinnati

Penn State began the season 4-0 and weren’t tested at all up to that point. Its closest victory came in a road win over Boston College by 11 points in Week 2, but the Nittany Lions team blew out their other opponents. However, that wasn’t the case in a Week 5 matchup against Cincinnati.

Late in the fourth quarter, the Nittany Lions trailed the Bearcats 17-14 but were driving down the field. After a short run by running back Steve Smith, the team lined up in i-formation from just outside the 5-yard line.

Quarterback John Shaffer handed it off to power back David Clark, who fought and plowed his way through defenders before carrying multiple Bearcats into the end zone, giving Penn State the 21-17 lead.

Despite Penn State’s defense getting picked apart throughout the game by Cincinnati quarterback Danny McCoin, the Nittany Lions held off the Bearcats for the final minutes, escaping with a 23-17 victory.

Statement win over No. 2 Alabama

Ranked No. 6 in the country, Penn State entered Tuscaloosa, Alabama with its first major test of the season.

Despite being ranked lower than the Crimson Tide, the Nittany Lions appeared to be the better team throughout the game, winning 23-3 while scoring the final 23 points of the game.

Penn State was led by two rushing touchdowns from D.J. Dozier and Blair Thomas, respectively, as the offensive line mauled a star-studded Alabama front seven. On the defensive end, the Nittany Lions shut down an offensive led by quarterback Mike Shula and running back Bobby Humphrey.

This game put the nation on notice that Penn State was a legitimate contender for the national title after traveling into Alabama and taking down the then-No. 2 Crimson Tide. The Nittany Lions took over that No. 2 for the next few weeks.

2-point conversion stop vs. Maryland

In another tight battle, Penn State needed a victory against a Maryland team who was 4-4 at the time to keep any title hopes alive.

Leading 17-9, the Nittany Lions needed one final stop to fend off a Maryland team who had an upset on its mind. The Terrapins marched down the field and scored a touchdown to pull to within two points of Penn State.

During a time period when ties could be the result of a game, a tie against Maryland could’ve been detrimental to championship hopes. But the Terrapins were still in need of a 2-point conversion to tie the game up.

Maryland lined up for a 2-point conversion with hopes of putting a damper on Penn State’s title run. It wouldn’t succeed, as defensive back Duffy Cobbs batted the ball down and the Nittany Lions came out on top 17-15, maintaining their perfect 9-0 record.

Gary Wilkerson’s game-saving hit vs. Notre Dame

Two weeks later, Penn State traveled to South Bend to take on rival Notre Dame. It was the first year under Lou Holtz, and while the Fighting Irish weren't necessarily having a strong season, they still showed signs of talent.

Those signs all came together to battle the No. 3 Nittany Lions in a tight game. Penn State was on the receiving end of some major breaks as a kickoff return touchdown from eventual 1987 Heisman Trophy winner Tim Brown was called back for a penalty. Brown also dropped a third-down pass later in the game that ended up being costly in Notre Dame’s upset hopes.

Despite this, Notre Dame still had a chance to win the game at the end of the fourth quarter. With third-and-goal from the 18-yard line, Notre Dame quarterback Steve Beuerlein dropped back and fired a pass to tight end Joel Williams in the end zone. But Penn State cornerback Gary Wilkerson popped Williams, breaking up the pass and saving the game for the Nittany Lions.

The fourth down play was completed but well short of the end zone, and Penn State held on to defeat Notre Dame, 24-19.

Fiesta Bowl victory versus Miami

After the victory over Notre Dame, it was smooth sailing to the Fiesta Bowl, where the Nittany Lions met a star-studded Miami team.

Led by 1986 Heisman Trophy winner Vinny Testaverde, Penn State had its hands full. However, the Nittany Lions took that underdog mentality to the Hurricanes.

Penn State’s defense completely shut down the nation’s top quarterback, as Testaverde threw five interceptions, none more important than the final one to close the game out.

With 18 seconds remaining, Testaverde had a chance to redeem his four picks from earlier in the game. His fourth down pass was thrown toward multiple blue jerseys and was picked off in the end zone by Pete Giftopoulos.

Penn State’s fifth interception of Testaverde sealed the deal of what was perceived to be an upset of the previously dominant Hurricane team. Penn State became the 1986 national champions with a 14-10 win over Miami.

MORE 40 YEAR ANNIVERSARY CONTENT

title: Centre County news outlets sue Penn State Board of Trustees over alleged First Amendment violations
author: Grace Levy | The Daily Collegian
description:
Centre County news outlets are suing the Penn State Board of Trustees over alleged First Amendment violations due to updated bylaws which they allege control what trustees say to the press and public.

The lawsuit was filed on Wednesday in the U.S. District Court for the Middle District of Pennsylvania by Spotlight PA, the Centre Daily Times and StateCollege.com.

The lawsuit alleges that bylaw amendments adopted by the Board of Trustees in November 2025 “effectively eliminated trustees’ freedom to communicate with Plaintiffs” and “severely restrict trustees’ speech rights.”

Trustee Code of Conduct Section 2.03 (c) of the amended bylaws, state “negative or critical public statements about the Board, the University or its students, alumni, community, faculty, staff, and other stakeholders do not serve the University’s interests and are inconsistent with a Trustee’s fiduciary obligation to act always in the best interests of the University.”

The lawsuit alleges this constitutes a “gag policy” and highly restricts trustee speech.

Additionally, the amended policies restrict trustees’ speech to the press by requiring them to seek and obtain advance approval of statements to the media, effectively allowing Board leadership to “pick and choose” what information and opinions are shared, according to the lawsuit.

Trustees are also subject to disciplinary action for going against these policies, according to the bylaws.

The lawsuit in turn alleges that the “Gag Policy” “unlawfully infringes trustees" First Amendment rights as speakers, and, in turn, infringes the First Amendment rights of Plaintiffs to receive such information from trustees who are "willing to speak" about Board matters but are "restrained from doing so."

Wyatt DuBois, a university spokesperson, said in an email to the Collegian that “the University generally does not comment on pending litigation.”

The Board of Trustees is not new to lawsuits regarding free speech, having been sued multiple times in the past few years by at least three plaintiffs. The Board was sued by former trustee Barry Fenchak in 2024, alleging defamation and retaliation.

Later in 2024, trustee Anthony Lubrano sued the Board, also alleging retaliation for comments made to the media regarding the renaming of Penn State’s Beaver Stadium.

Spotlight PA, who is a plaintiff in the current lawsuit against the Board, previously sued the Board in 2023 for violations of Pennsylvania’s Sunshine Act. The Sunshine Act requires publication agencies to act and deliberate in public meetings that are open to the general public.

This case was settled in June 2025 and the Board was required to disclose more information about closed-door meetings and complete trainings on open meetings law.

MORE CRIME AND COURTS COVERAGE

title: Column | Assessing the job security of every Penn State Athletics coach
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
Correction: A previous version of this article referred to goaltender Josh Fleming as Julian Fleming. The Daily Collegian apologizes for this error.

Pat Kraft doesn’t blink when it’s time to shake the tree. This year alone, Happy Valley waved goodbye to legends like men’s volleyball’s Mark Pavlik via retirement and men’s gymnastics’ longtime boss Randy Jepson by a change of leadership.

As Penn State Athletics has some fresh, new faces and plenty of old hands still hanging on, it’s time to size up just how hot each coach’s seat is after this past season.

Kieger line

Kraft’s pink slip for women’s basketball coach Carolyn Kieger proved there are only two ways to get canned at Penn State: stick around long enough for your results to flatline, or give the brass a reason to believe the program needs a serious shakeup.

With that in mind, I see four coaches sitting below what I’ll call the Kieger line — having been seated long enough in the saddle for the axe to be a possibility when the wins don’t follow the investment.

Mike Rhoades

After three full seasons in charge, the results haven't been anything to be optimistic about on the basketball court for men’s coach Rhoades' unit.

Give him as much credit as you want, but it’s hard to deny his tendency to underdeliver. His rosters consistently feature promising recruits who have competitive potential, yet he can’t get that talent to stay; take Kayden Mingo, the program's top one of all time who left after a year, for example.

While the portal may have been underutilized, it seems like that’s been a main avenue for roster-building this season.

If Rhoades can’t steer clear of another train wreck like last year — coming dead last in the Big Ten, which is no small feat — he’ll be packing his bags soon enough.

With Kieger already out the door, fans are watching Rhoades like a hawk, waiting to see if he’s next on the chopping block. And talks of who would go first between him and Kieger already heated up at the midpoint of last year.

Matteo Zennaro

Zennaro’s fencing program has slammed into a brick wall. Neither the men’s nor the women’s squad got a whiff of the NCAA top ten, which is baffling for a program with this kind of historical record.

Toss in last offseason’s misconduct allegations, and Zennaro’s seat is heating up fast.

Guy Gadowsky

Gadowsky took Penn State hockey from a pipe dream to primetime, anchoring the athletic department as a more established program at the school. Still, after over a decade, he can’t get this team past the big dogs. It’s starting to look like James Franklin’s tenure: noise and sizzle but barely enough substance to go any further than that.

Last year’s goalie situation with Kevin Reidler at times playing over Josh Fleming was a real head-scratcher, but Gadowsky kept things steady otherwise. Maybe patience pays off, and he finally brings a trophy to Happy Valley.

And I’m not calling for Gadowsky’s head. There’s nobody better for the gig so far, but it’s fair to wonder if something has got to give. If even a generational DI prospect (the most expensive one, too) like Gavin McKenna couldn’t move the needle, you have to start asking some less than ideal questions.

Real evaluations of this era’s wasted talent need to be had — and I’ll be dying on that hill.

Safe

Mike Gambino

Gambino’s seat is as chilly as State College winter, as things currently stand.

His first two years were solid, and this season’s bumps aren’t enough to send him packing. If things keep sliding, maybe the athletic department will poke around. But of all the tenured coaches, Gambino’s future is the biggest wild card.

As he’s in the midst of building something, pulling the plug now would be a bone-headed move, so the arrow’s still pointing up.

Alexandra McIntyre

McIntyre has led the women’s tennis program since 2019, and her trajectory has been mostly upward.

In 2025, she steered the Nittany Lions to a program-record 14 wins. Tennis might fly under the radar at Penn State, but McIntyre has built something special with little fanfare and even less budget.

Kayla Treanor

In her debut season, Treanor blew expectations out of the water. She landed one of the best freshmen the program’s ever seen with Rocquette Allen and kept the top talent already on board.

She’s already proved herself as one of Kraft’s best hires.

Hollie Bonewit-Cron

Swimming and diving might not grab headlines in Happy Valley, but Bonewit-Cron made waves in her first two seasons, making her hire pay off right away.

While the program probably won’t be hoisting trophies anytime soon, she’s steadily raised the bar and given fans a reason to feel good about the future in the pool.

Kristen Simpson

Three years in, Simpson has been a steady hand on the links. She’s helped her golfers trim strokes and sharpen their game.

In women’s golf, she’s a rare blend of youth and Power Four experience. With Penn State’s golf resources on the rise, expect Simpson to stick around for the long haul.

Bruce Lipka

Like McIntyre, Lipka is fighting an uphill battle with Penn State’s men’s tennis program. Still, with over thirty years of coaching under his belt, he has already moved the trajectory in just two seasons.

If you know the tennis programs are small, you know Lipka is a huge asset. He has sent players in his career to Grand Slams and developed some of the nation’s best, which is a great mind to have at the helm. Expectations should be realistic, but Lipka is the right guy to chase those goals.

Mark Leon

Leon bleeds blue and white.

He’s a former Nittany Lion golfer and assistant coach, so nobody knows the program better. This is only his second head coaching job, so he’s still learning the ropes, but that homegrown knowledge will serve him well. There’s no reason to think his seat is anything but safe.

Sarah Brown

Nine years in, Brown has found her groove with women’s gymnastics. The Nittany Lions made the NCAA regionals four straight years, so I can’t ask for much better there. With that foundation in place, Brown’s job is as safe as they come.

Clarisa Crowell

Crowell has turned Penn State softball upside down, or rather rightside up. She’s landed some of the best freshmen ever and steered the team to its first NCAA tournament in over a decade.

Crowell built a juggernaut on and off the diamond, churning out tough, talented alumni, cementing herself as a Happy Valley icon.

Jeff Tambroni

Jeff Tambroni has worked wonders at Penn State, turning the men’s lacrosse squad into a team that can go toe-to-toe with the Ivy League and the nation’s best. Calling for a coaching change would be downright foolish, as Tambroni keeps delivering.

Erica Dambach

A notch safer than the men’s lacrosse coach is women’s soccer boss Dambach, who’s been a fixture in Happy Valley for decades. And even after a down year, she’s still the perfect fit for Penn State.

Firing her, or even thinking about it, would be a blunder. Nobody loves the university or understands the women’s soccer blueprint better than Dambach. It’s just a matter of waiting for the cycle to swing back around, and she’s the only one who knows how to get them there.

Jeff Kampersal

Kampersal’s story is a lot like Gadowsky’s: he’s built Penn State women’s hockey into a national contender, even if the trophy case is still collecting dust. (Conference banners are nice, but the big one remains just a tad away.)

Kampersal is an ace at turning raw recruits into stars. Last year’s team was built to chase a national title, and even if this year was their best shot, they’ll be back in the hunt soon.

John Gondak

Gondak has been running the show in Happy Valley for decades, racking up experience as both coach and assistant. He’s one of the sharpest minds in Penn State athletics.

The track program has thrived under his watch, pulling in top talent and competing nationally. Losing Gondak would be a gut punch to the program’s prestige.

Katie Schumacher-Cawley

Schumacher-Cawley is the second-safest coach at Penn State, and her run with women’s volleyball has been nothing short of miraculous. Even with a down year during their National Championship push, most of the problems came from off the court, not from Schumacher-Cawley.

What Schumacher-Cawley’s done with the program is flat-out brilliant. She’s an icon in Penn State sports and a leader who’ll keep the team near the top. Firing her is nowhere near being on the table.

Cael Sanderson

Sanderson is the gold standard for job security. If he’s not already the best coach on campus, he’s in the running for best in the country.

He’s already an all-time great, and he’s turned Penn State wrestling into a trophy factory where other programs can only compete for second place. Even thinking about kicking Sanderson would be pure lunacy.

New hires

Talking about job security for the fresh crop of new Penn State Athletics hires would be encouraging you to worry about something completely pointless.

So instead, here’s a cheat sheet for a timeframe on when those tough questions might start to form.

Men’s gymnastics’ Tony Beck: likely 4-season cushion

Men’s soccer’s Rob Dow: 3 seasons

Women’s basketball’s Tanisha Wright: 3 seasons

Football’s Matt Campbell: 2 seasons

Field hockey’s Hannah Prince: 3 seasons

Men’s volleyball’s Colin McMillan: 2 seasons

MORE SPORTS CONTENT

title: Penn State track and field's Hayley Kitching breaks 800m school record
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After setting the 800-meter school record in 2024, Hayley Kitching has done it again.

Kitching broke the school record during the 800m prelims at the NCAA East First Round on Wednesday, running 1:59.60 to finish first and book her ticket to the finals on Saturday.

The Coffs Harbour, Australia, native is coming off her fifth career Big Ten title earlier this month, where she ran 2:01.77 to take home the title.

During the outdoor season, Kitching ran her first sub-two minute 800m, setting the indoor school record with a time of 1:59.22. She also has the 600-meter school record in 1:26.97, the 1,000-meter school record in 2:38.45 and the 1,500-meter record in 4:05.81.

title: Penn State women's basketball earns commitment from Mountain West Freshman of the Year Skylar Durley
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Coach Tanisha Wright has earned one of her biggest commitments yet for Penn State.

The Lady Lions earned a commitment from former Nevada guard Skylar Durley on Thursday, marking the ninth player on their 2026-27 squad.

Durley, a 5-foot-9 guard from Oklahoma City, started her first year in the NCAA on a high note. The freshman phenom started in all 31 games, averaging 11.8 points and six rebounds per game.

Following her first season with the Wolfpack, Durley was named Mountain West Freshman of the Year after being named freshman of the week five times throughout the season. She's the first player in Nevada history to earn that honor since it joined the Mountain West in 2010.

Durley scored double digits in 20 games throughout her freshman year, including a career-high 22 points against Utah State and a career-high 14 rebounds against Wyoming.

MORE BASKETBALL COVERAGE

title: Penn State men's basketball promotes Brent Scott to AHC, makes more moves in roster update
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State released its first roster for the 2026-27 season on its website Thursday.

Most notably, Brent Scott was promoted to associate head coach after serving as an assistant coach for the past three seasons. The Nittany Lions also made a few more changes to the staff in addition to adding another walk-on to the roster and posting measurements for players.

Scott has been key in the development of centers during his time in Happy Valley. He guided Yanic Konan Niederhauser into becoming a first-round NBA draft pick in 2025, and Ivan Jurić had a standout freshman year last season, averaging 10.2 points and 5.3 rebounds per game.

The associate head coach role was open this offseason as Jamal Brunt departed the program to be an assistant coach at Syracuse. Mike Rhoades stayed in house for Brunt's replacement with Scott.

Meanwhile, the Nittany Lions hired Sam Little to the "video coordinator/player development" position. Little spent the past two seasons at Western Michigan under current Penn State assistant coach Dwayne Stephens as an assistant coach/video coordinator.

Jake Szczecina was Penn State's video coordinator for the past three seasons, but he's now the director of basketball operations. Since 2023, that role was held by Patrick Dorney, who's now the team's chief of staff.

Additionally, the Nittany Lions added freshman walk-on guard Grant Spacciapolli to their roster. Spacciapolli played at Pine-Richland High School in Pennsylvania, and he joins sophomore Chris Lotito as walk-ons for Penn State.

Along with the addition of Spacciapolli, the Nittany Lions released listed measurements for each player. Guard Thomas Allard is 6-foot-7 and 210 pounds, guard Roberts Blums is 6-foot-4 and 190 pounds, wing Brant Byers is 6-foot-8 and 200 pounds, forward Tim Oboh is 6-foot-11 and 230 pounds and guard Jay Rodgers is 6-foot-3 and 185 pounds.

Guard Andy Gemao is 6-foot-1, forward Aleksandar Zecevic is 6-foot-9 and Spacciapolli is 6-foot-3, but none of them have listed weights yet.

Jurić has the same measurements as last season at 7-foot and 250 pounds, and so does guard Reggie Grodin at 6-foot-5 and 200 pounds and Lotito at 6-foot-7 and 215 pounds.

No jersey numbers were posted on the new roster update. International commits Roko Prkačin and François Wibaut don't appear on this version of the roster as they have yet to officially sign with Penn State.

title: Penn State track and field earns several qualifying marks at NCAA East First Round
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Everything was on the line as Penn State traveled to Lexington, Kentucky, for the NCAA East First Round, awaiting the results of 32 athletes with a chance to qualify for the NCAA Championships.

The Nittany Lions competed across four days of regionals from Wednesday to Saturday, booking 10 tickets to the NCAA Championship.

Wednesday

Graduate student Collin Burkhart opened the action in the men’s shot put on Wednesday, looking to earn his first trip to the NCAA Championship in his final collegiate season.

After throws of 66.32 meters and 67.14 meters in his first two attempts, Burkhart threw for 67.42 meters with his final chance, earning the sixth-best mark and his first-ever ticket to the big stage.

In his first collegiate season, freshman Nolan Carey qualified for the regional meet with the second-best javelin throw in school history. However, he finished one place shy of a championship berth, throwing 66.08 meters for 13th place.

Penn State earned another pair of NCAA Championship tickets in the men’s shot put, where graduate student Dylan Lambrecht set the No. 5 mark in school history with a throw of 19.58 meters. Following his second place throw, senior Ryan Henry also qualified for the big stage, throwing 19.02 meters for fifth, while freshman Bryce Thompson finished in 21st with 18.26 meters.

After missing the NCAA Championship by one place during the indoor season, junior Nick Sloff found himself one step closer to qualifying in the men’s 1,500-meters, running 3:43.11 to finish second in the prelims and earn a spot in Friday’s finals.

Also competing on Friday is sophomore Ajani Dwyer, who set a school record during the 100-meter prelims. Dwyer ran 9.98 seconds for another second place finish, setting a new record for the third time this season.

In the men’s 400-meters, Jake Palermo had a bounce-back performance after failing to qualify for the Big Ten Championship finals earlier this month.

Palermo ran 45.25 seconds at the NCAA East First Round to finish fifth and book Penn State’s third ticket to Friday’s final round.

Penn State’s “800m U” legacy continued in the regional meet on Wednesday, as four athletes qualified for the men’s 800-meter finals.

Senior Niko Schultz led the way with a time of 1:46.17 for second place, while reigning Big Ten champion Allon Clay finished in third in 1:46.55. Yukichi Ishii qualified after missing out on the Big Ten finals, running 1:46.91 for ninth place, while junior Andrew Regnier earned the last spot for the Nittany Lions in 1:47.03 for 11th place.

Kaleb Nastari missed a spot in the finals, finishing in 31st place in 1:48.55.

Thursday

Senior Alex Pancoast kicked off the action on Thursday in the women’s hammer throw, finishing in 40th place with a mark of 55.58 meters.

The Nittany Lions continued the action on the field, where Audrey Friedman finished 29th overall in the women’s javelin, throwing for 46.65 meters.

In the women’s shot put, sophomore Sasha Garnett booked Penn State’s first NCAA Championship ticket of the day, throwing 16.88 meters for 10th place — tied for the third-best throw in school history. Freshman Skye Petrie-Cameron finished in 28th place with 15.87 meters.

Penn State’s track action got started in the women’s 1,500m, where senior Lea Hatcher finished 39th overall with a mark of 4:21.61. Like Hatcher, fellow senior Marie-Eloise Leclair also missed out on the finals in the 100m, running 11.42 seconds for 32nd place.

The Nittany Lions turned the page in the women’s 800m, as three athletes booked their spots in Saturday’s finals with a school record performance.

Senior Hayley Kitching led the way with a first place finish in 1:59.60, marking her first sub-two-minute performance of the season, breaking her previous school record set in 2024. Freshman Boh Ritchie also qualified for finals, running 2:04.20 for 18th place, followed by Allison Johnson in 2:04.55 for 23rd place. Sophomore Tayissa Buchanan just missed the finals, running 2:05.23 for 28th place.

Despite falling short of a finals berth in the 100m, Leclair came back and ran the fastest 200-meter in Penn State history since 2014, qualifying for finals in 14th place with a time of 23.00 seconds, the third-best in school history.

Senior Claire Daniels wrapped up Thursday's races with the 10,000-meters, where she ran 34:51.66 for 34th place.

Friday

The Nittany Lions opened up the action on Friday with the men’s high jump, where freshman Trip Campbell and senior Robert Allen fell shy of qualifying for the NCAA Championships. Campbell finished in 17th place with a mark of 2.11 meters, while Allen finished 34th with 2.06 meters.

In the men’s 1,500m finals, Sloff came back from his second-place finish in the prelims and earned his first championship appearance, running 3:43.33 for 16th place.

Senior Ryan Watson took the track for his second straight NCAA East First Round 3,000-meter steeplechase appearance. However, he fell far short of a championship ticket, running 9:13.48 for 39th place.

Dwyer’s success continued in the 100m as he finished third in the finals, running 9.99 seconds for his second straight sub-10 second performance.

While Palermo broke the 51-year-old school record in the 400m finals with a time of 45.19 seconds, he too fell short of a championship berth, finishing in 14th place.

Penn State’s 800m success continued in the men’s 800m finals, where Clay took home the victory with a facility record. The Fujisawa, Kanagawa, Japan, native ran 1:44.63 to qualify for finals, followed by Schultz in 1:46.01 for eighth place and Ishii in 1:46.48 for 11th place — both qualifying for the championships.

In addition to Regnier missing out on the championships with a 1:47.92 for 21st place, the men’s 4x400m squad also missed out on a ticket, running 3:08.52 for 23rd place.

Saturday

The final day of qualifying kicked off with sophomore Hannah Riolo competing in the sole field event of the day for the Nittany Lions. Riolo jumped 1.76 meters in the women’s high jump, finishing in 14th place.

In the women’s steeplechase, senior Allison Dougherty finished in 32nd place, running a time of 10:24.36.

Penn State’s 800m success continued into the final day as Kitching broke the school record for the second time this week, running 1:59.15 to finish third and qualify for the NCAA Championships. Joining her with record-breaking marks was Johnson, who ran 2:02.65 for 15th place and the No. 4 mark all-time and Ritchie, who ran 2:03.30 for 17th place and the No. 6 mark all-time.

Leclair took the line for the final time in her Penn State career in the 200m finals, running 23.06 seconds for 18th place.

Up next

Ten Nittany Lions will compete at the NCAA Championships in Eugene, Oregon, from June 10 to June 13.

title: Film review | 3-star TE Sean Currie brings height advantage, athleticism to Penn State’s 2027 class
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State earned its second tight end commitment in the Class of 2027 when 3-star Sean Currie announced his commitment on Friday afternoon via the Rivals YouTube channel.

Currie ranks as the No. 15 player out of New Jersey and No. 32 tight end in the 2027 class per 247Sports composite. He had a strong junior season at The Lawrenceville School, closing out the year with 34 receptions for 474 yards and eight touchdowns. He also played some edge, recording three sacks and two tackles for loss.

Additionally, Currie’s background as a former highly regarded lacrosse recruit lends to him being an elite athlete, as he possesses not only the sheer size of being 6-foot-7 and 230 pounds, but also the mobility that’s required at the tight end position.

His film showcases his ability to use his size to his advantage by snagging passes that shorter targets might not, while making his presence felt on the line as a blocker.

A towering frame that remains mobile

Currie towers over many of his adversaries, and he uses that height for his benefit. In one-on-one matchups, his wingspan makes it easier to get separation. He also has a longer reach toward the ball, allowing him to make catches over his shoulders and out in front of him more comfortably against smaller competition.

As it can be more difficult to maneuver in the same ways that a smaller player can, size of that degree can become a weakness. But Currie has rare speed and agility for his size that beats defenders off the snap, and he works himself into open space up the middle and along the sidelines.

Currie is an elite route runner that attacks open space with a swiftness unbecoming of his size, and once he's got the ball with nothing but green grass in front of him, he's tough to track down. pic.twitter.com/xtVtKupGCY— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

Covering him is also difficult on passing plays because of Currie’s jumping capabilities. He has strong legs and swiftly reaches his peak, and his soft hands make him a reliable receiver against noticeably shorter cornerbacks and safeties.

Once he’s got the ball with room to run, his long strides can carry him into the end zone. But even with remaining opposition, Currie has no problem bulldozing through for extra yardage.

When attempting to tackle him, any solo attempt above the waist is a no-go, forcing the knees and ankles to be the only method of taking him down.

Currie is an elite route runner that attacks open space with a swiftness unbecoming of his size, and once he's got the ball with nothing but green grass in front of him, he's tough to track down. pic.twitter.com/xtVtKupGCY— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

Safe hands from all areas

Currie is a fundamentally sound pass catcher that won’t drop the early ones, something that can help him thrive in the college game.

Passes often come around his hips rather than at his chest, being a larger target than the majority of options. However, Currie consistently secures the ball with safe hands and isn’t afraid to bend over to make a catch before slamming into contact.

As a taller target, sometimes passes come a little low. But he's comfortable catching from any range. Currie also possesses grit and isn't afraid to hit the turf if it means getting into the end zone. pic.twitter.com/Aetldvlzcr— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

Currie’s long wingspan also allows him more space for the quarterback to throw a leading pass out in front of him. When that pass is slightly too far ahead, Currie gets creative with how he adapts — and he has previously made flashy one-handed catches.

Currie is also a creative pass catcher and will attempt one-handed grabs that have ended in flashy touchdowns in the past, featuring his ability to adapt on the fly and make the most out of any pass.Here's a pair of touchdowns showcasing his one-handed ability: pic.twitter.com/2jW9ohSjO9— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

He’s at his best when the pass is higher, as he can easily get up for a grab. Currie will bring down previously uncatchable passes because of his natural gift of size and comfortability with his tall frame.

Currie is also proficient in catching over his shoulder and is a quality route runner that works himself into open spaces for uncontested receptions. He’s especially a threat in the back corner of the end zone on corner routes after gathering speed while in motion before the snap.

On one-on-one matchups, Currie's strength and wingspan allow him to bully opponents for separation, making open catches in front of him as well as over his shoulder when he beats the defensive back longwise. pic.twitter.com/6LS3MzjAo4— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

A developing blocker

Currie improved his blocking capabilities greatly between his sophomore and junior seasons, according to 247Sports’ Director of Scouting Andrew Ivins. That enhanced technique pairs nicely with his size, making him a threat when he helps open a path for his running backs.

Currie is a powerful blocker that pushes his opponent back more often than not. He’s strong on initial contact and uses his size to carry momentum forward for leverage when he gets underneath his matchup’s arms.

In the blocking game, Currie is quick to the point of contact and plays his angles well, often using leverage from attacking opposing flanks to bulldoze a path for his backs. pic.twitter.com/YAhyaYWGLA— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

He’s also smart with his angles and possesses great vision when deciding who to block. Currie will step back, maneuver to the strong side and plan an extra step ahead in his progression to see who on the defense is able to make a move on the ball carrier.

In addition, Currie goes through progressions well when figuring out who to block. When he recognizes that the first layer is secure, he fills the gap to block the secondary and gives his backs extra yardage. pic.twitter.com/kkHpXpN8KU— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

Currie’s ability to recognize where he’s needed at those particular moments creates additional space for running backs and can be the difference between a short gain and a first down.

When he’s not the target downfield, Currie will continue to block after the play is made and keep defenders at bay while his teammates reap the extra yards. There have been times when Currie’s lead blocking has resulted in a touchdown on plays where it wasn’t necessarily guaranteed.

Currie, when not the target on a passing play, continues to make an impact in the blocking game downfield by providing key blocks on extended plays en route to the end zone.Here's Currie fending off a group of trailing defenders while his teammate scores: pic.twitter.com/2yoBoHSEJM— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

title: Conference breakdown | Ranking Big Ten football programs heading into summer
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Big Ten has produced the national champion the last three years, proving the success of the conference year after year.

Surely, 2026 should be no different, as several teams sit atop the conference and are regarded highly by the nation heading into the summer. Here’s a look at where they currently stack up.

Tier 1: Ohio State, Indiana, Oregon

While Ohio State missed out on winning back-to-back national titles when it fell to Miami in the College Football Playoff quarterfinals last year, the Buckeyes aren’t expected to fall far from last year’s position.

Redshirt sophomore quarterback Julian Sayin will take the reins for his second year as a starter. He will still be throwing to star wide receiver Jeremiah Smith, who is a strong Heisman Trophy candidate and will likely be suiting up for his final season with the Buckeyes.

One of Ohio State’s biggest challengers is reigning national champion Indiana. While the Hoosiers are without Heisman-winning quarterback Fernando Mendoza, they have many other pieces of its squad returning, including wide receiver Charlie Becker and left tackle Carter Smith, who won Big Ten Offensive Lineman of the Year.

Indiana also attacked the transfer portal, bringing in former Nittany Lion cornerback A.J. Harris and TCU quarterback Josh Hoover to take over Mendoza’s position.

Oregon also had a big run in the playoffs last season, falling to the Hoosiers in the Peach Bowl semifinal. Considering quarterback Dante Moore is back for his final season and is joined by speedy wide receiver Evan Stewart, the Ducks could make some noise in the Big Ten this year.

Tier 2: Michigan, Penn State, USC, Washington

A new era of football has emerged at Michigan, with former Utah head coach Kyle Whittingham replacing Sherrone Moore. Sophomore quarterback and former No. 1 overall recruit Bryce Underwood is set to start for his second season, hopefully growing his game under a new coaching staff after a freshman campaign that left much to be desired.

Likewise, Penn State is entering its first season under a new coach for the first time since 2014. However, Matt Campbell has built a complete roster with dozens of transfers from Iowa State, including quarterback Rocco Becht, running back Carson Hansen and wide receivers Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen.

The Nittany Lions were just one game away from playing for the national championship two seasons ago, and they still have a few players from that squad, like Tony Rojas, Zion Tracy and Audavion Collins. But with plenty of change since, Penn State is simply looking to make the most out of Year 1 under Campbell.

USC was one of six Big Ten programs to finish in the AP poll, and is expected to stay similar in strength to last year’s 9-4 record. The Trojans return a majority of its roster, including its entire offensive line, and have former TCU head coach Gary Patterson — TCU’s all-time winningest coach — as defensive coordinator.

While Washington made the national championship in 2023, the departure of Kalen DeBoer and its key players from that run has resulted in somewhat of a rebuilding phase with six and nine wins the last two years, respectively.

But with Demond Williams Jr. entering his second year as the starting quarterback and talent out wide with Dezmen Roebuck, the Huskies could have a strong upcoming season in the Big Ten if Williams puts it all together.

Tier 3: UCLA, Iowa, Illinois

UCLA is a rising star in the Big Ten after upsetting Penn State last season and winning the next two games. While it only finished with those three wins on the season, things are expected to be better under new coach Bob Chesney with dual-threat quarterback Nico Iamaleava returning.

Iowa lost starting quarterback Mark Gronowski, who ran out of eligibility, but has two former transfers set to battle it out in Jeremy Hecklinski and Hank Brown. It's unclear who will win the job, but on the other side of the ball, the Hawkeyes' defense is projected to be their strong suit as always.

While Illinois has to adjust to life without starting quarterback Luke Altmyer, there is plenty of young talent on the roster. However, it’s up to them to put the pieces together to continue building off two straight successful campaigns.

Tier 4: Nebraska, Northwestern, Minnesota

Nebraska underwent a significant change during the offseason when two-year starting quarterback Dylan Raiola entered the transfer portal and landed at Oregon. Now, UNLV transfer Anthony Colandrea will likely lead the way for the Cornhuskers in a year that could make or officially break Matt Rhule’s tenure in Lincoln.

While Northwestern is unveiling its state-of-the-art Ryan Field in October, the new stadium might not see too many wins from its home team. Under new offensive coordinator Chip Kelly, the Wildcats’ offense is expected to take a step forward, but likely won’t see too much growth as an overall team.

Minnesota is one of the few teams in the Big Ten who didn’t experience much roster turnover or changes this season and will likely stay similar to last year’s 8-5 record.

Tier 5: Rutgers, Wisconsin, Maryland, Michigan State, Purdue

Rutgers, Wisconsin, Maryland, Michigan State and Purdue each recorded two or less conference wins last season, but there’s hope on the horizon for Wisconsin and Michigan State.

The Spartans have a new coach in Pat Fitzgerald, and the Badgers invested more resources into their program this offseason, so both could see improvements from last year. It’s unlikely, however, that they’ll be competing among the best of the Big Ten.

title: Penn State track and field books 32 entries to NCAA East First Round
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After less-than-favorable outcomes at the Big Ten championships last week, Penn State is off to the next part of its postseason.

The Nittany Lions will compete with the top-48 athletes in each event from Wednesday to Saturday for the top 12 spots and a ticket to the NCAA Championship next month.

Here’s a look at the Nittany Lions who will be competing this week for a spot in the NCAA Championships.

Sprints

For the first time in several years, Penn State has a woman’s entry in the 100-meter and 200-meter at the NCAA East First Round. Marie-Eloise Leclair is seeded in the top-20 in both races, with the first round set for 6 p.m. and 8:45 p.m. on Thursday.

Leclair would have to get through two rounds of action for a spot in the top 24, with the finals slated for 6:35 p.m. and 7:50 p.m. on Saturday. She’s seeded 16th in the 100m and 13th in the 200m — both seeds near a championship appearance — so she’ll have to stay true to her personal bests of 11.12 seconds and 22.75 seconds for her ticket.

On the men’s side, sophomore Ajani Dwyer is set for his first NCAA East First Round in the 100m, with the prelims at 7 p.m. on Wednesday and finals at 6:35 p.m. on Friday.

He’s seeded with a time of 9.96 seconds and the fourth-best mark in the region, but ran an even better time of 9.93 seconds at the Big Ten Championship last weekend.

In the men’s 400-meters, sophomore Jake Palermo is seeded 13th in the region ahead of his prelims at 7:45 p.m. on Wednesday. However, he’s coming off one of the worst performances of his career, when he ran 46.75 seconds and was eliminated in the first round of the conference championship.

Palermo is also part of the men’s 4x400m relay at 8:45 p.m. on Friday, running alongside Carter Fitzgerald, Matt DeMatteo and Peyton Sommers. They’re ranked No. 23 in the region with a time of 3:05.39, and will look for a top-12 finish for a spot on the biggest stage.

Mid-distance/distance

Penn State’s mid-distance success has been on display all season, and that will be showcased with nine athletes in the men’s and women’s 800-meters.

The men’s 800m will kick off at 7:50 p.m. Wednesday and conclude with the finals at 7:05 p.m. on Friday. Big Ten champion Allon Clay leads the way with the No. 1 seed, followed by Niko Schultz in third, Andrew Regnier in eighth, Yukichi Ishii in 10th and Kaleb Nastari in 37th.

On the women’s side, they’ll race at 7:50 p.m. on Thursday and 7:05 p.m. on Saturday. Big Ten champion Hayley Kitching sits 12th in the region, followed by Allison Johnson in 19th, Boh Ritchie in 21st and Tayissa Buchanan in 29th.

Junior Nick Sloff will kick off the men’s 1,500-meters at 6:30 p.m. on Wednesday with the finals at 5:15 p.m. on Friday. He comes into the race with the No. 18 mark at 3:39.56.

In the women’s 1,500m, senior Lea Hatcher is ranked No. 46 in the region and will compete at 6:30 p.m. on Thursday and 5:15 p.m. on Saturday.

Senior Claire Daniels has Penn State’s lone 10,000-meter entry, racing at 9:10 p.m. on Thursday. She has the 19th-best mark with a season-best 33:18.60.

Ryan Watson earned a spot in the men’s 3,000-meter steeplechase at 5:40 p.m. on Friday with the No. 22 seed, while Allison Dougherty has a spot in the women’s steeplechase at 5:40 p.m. on Saturday with the No. 41 seed.

Jumps

Penn State has a trio of athletes qualified to compete in the high jump and another athlete in the pole vault this week.

Freshman Trip Campbell is seeded with the No. 32 mark in the men’s high jump, competing at 2 p.m. on Saturday. His mark is just ahead of teammate Robert Allen’s mark of 2.12 meters, which is the No. 42 seed.

In the women’s high jump, sophomore Hannah Riolo is seeded 13th with a season-best mark of 1.80 meters — the seventh-best in school history — and will compete at 2 p.m. on Saturday.

Senior Tristan McGarrah just made the qualifying mark in the men’s pole vault, jumping 5.20 meters for the 46th-best mark in the region. He’ll likely need a big personal best to qualify for the NCAA Championships, when he competes at 6:30 p.m. on Wednesday.

Throws

In Darrell Hill’s first season at the helm with Penn State throws, he has nine athletes qualified for the NCAA East First Round.

Graduate student Collin Burkhart will kick off the throwing portion of the competition in the men’s hammer throw at 10 a.m. on Wednesday. He has the 12th-best mark in the region with 67.53 meters, good for No. 3 in school history.

Freshman Nolan Carey will have his shot at qualifying for the NCAA Championships in the men’s javelin at 1 p.m. on Friday, boasting the No. 6 mark ahead of the competition.

In the men’s shot put, the Nittany Lions have three athletes in the top-20 and in position to qualify for the NCAA Championships when they compete at 6:30 p.m. on Wednesday. Senior Ryan Henry leads the way with the No. 6 mark in 19.48 meters, followed by Dylan Lambrecht in seventh and freshman Bryce Thompson with the No. 16 mark.

Senior Alex Pancoast will kick off the women’s throws at 10 a.m. on Thursday, where she has the No. 36 mark in the conference. She’ll be followed by Audrey Friedman at 1 p.m. in the javelin throw, where she has the 31st-best mark.

The final throws event comes in the women’s shot put, where freshman Skye Petrie-Cameron will compete as the No. 17 seed and sophomore Sasha Garnett as the No. 23 seed. The two will throw at 6:30 p.m. on Thursday.

title: Penn State baseball's Mason Butash enters transfer portal
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Another Nittany Lion will look elsewhere for a team next season.

Right-handed pitcher Mason Butash announced his intentions to enter the transfer portal on Monday via X.

I am entering the transfer portal from Penn State with 1 year of eligibility going into my senior year. Butash has played the past three seasons with Penn State. He has a career 4-7 record with a 7.77 ERA.

This past year, Butash made 14 appearances and tallied 22 strikeouts across 28 innings pitched. He has one season of eligibility remaining.

Butash joins outfielder Jack Porter, pitcher Harrison Lollin, pitcher Matthew VanOstenbridge and catcher Nate Voss as Nittany Lions who are headed to the portal.

title: Penn State baseball sophomore pitcher Harrison Lollin enters transfer portal
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a rough year on the mound, the blue and white took another beating.

The Nittany Lions posted the Big Ten's worst ERA last season, and last year's blue and white leader in the stat, Harrison Lollin, announced his decision to enter the transfer portal.

The Monroe Township, New Jersey, native was a former top-ten recruit from his state, but struggled during his freshman year with an ERA over 10. This season, however, Lollin experienced a return to form, with 11 total appearances and a 4.05 ERA.

This end-of-year figure marked the only blue and white arm in the regular bullpen to record an ERA under five. Lollin uses an arsenal of five pitches, a low 90s fastball, a cutter, slider, curveball and changeup, which gave him a much larger selection than last year's bullpen options.

Lollin now looks for a change of scenery for the remainder of his collegiate career and to build off that already strong opportunity.

title: Penn State baseball left-handed reliever Matthew VanOstenbridge enters transfer portal
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
The struggles continue for the blue and white into the offseason.

Sophomore pitcher Matthew VanOstenbridge announced on social media his intention to enter the transfer portal.

I’m entering the transfer portal from Penn State with 2 years of eligibility remaining.LHPCumulative stats:51.2 ip 5.23 era 52k’s 26BB’s .238 oba 36 appearances Trackman data availableOpen to all opportunities! @FlatgroundApp @TreadHQ @prospectdugout @DanCevette… pic.twitter.com/W3P6PRHlep— Matthew VanOstenbridge (@MattyV_41) May 25, 2026

The Sinking Spring, Pennsylvania, native joins batters Nate Voss and Jack Porter and fellow arm Harrison Lollin as Nittany Lions to announce their intentions to transfer so far.

VanOstenbridge was a top-ten lefty and top-50 player in Pennsylvania coming out of high school, and over his true freshman year, he matched the hype. He notched a 4.32 ERA over 41.2 innings of work in his rookie season, with 38 strikeouts.

Yet for Penn State, a unit that struggled with the worst conference ERA last season, VanOstenbridge regressed with a 9 ERA over only ten innings of work. Those struggles were apparent for the sophomore, with 13 runs and 12 walks over that set of work, yet now he heads to a new home for a chance at a bounce-back year.
